text: The robotic arms of the da Vinci system are used to perform a primary closure of an incisional hernia. Mesh is placed over the hernia site and secured with trans-fascial sutures and tacks.

The surgeon uses sharp scissors without electrocautery to carefully dissect adhesions between the small bowel and fascia, utilizing the HD-III-D enhanced visualization of the robotic platform to distinguish tissue planes.

The surgeon uses a Cartier grasper in the left hand, visible on the left side of the screen, to atraumatically handle the intestines. As the dissection moves away from the intestines, electrocautery is applied to vascular fibro fatty adhesions. The primary closure of the incisional hernia is performed using a V-lock polypropylene suture, which is a barbed suture that securely holds tension along its length and facilitates securing to the fascia without knotting.
The surgeon begins the primary repair by placing a figure-of-eight suture and pulls it through, securing it to itself using the loop at the end of the V-lock polypropylene suture.

A variety of different options present themselves with robotic incisional hernia repair. Since the instruments are so dextrous, virtually any type of repair can be carried out. I've performed simple, straightforward mesh repairs as you would do with a standard laparoscopic approach. I've sewn the mesh in place around its circumference. or tacked it in place with trans-fascial sutures, and as in this case, started with primary closure of the defect. It's unclear yet which is the best with regards to patient recovery and recurrence rates. These studies simply have not been completed. Since a number of recurrences do occur as a result of mesh migrating out through large defects, it would seem that a primary closure of the fascia would be advantageous.

The surgeon places several throws of the suture through the fascia, pulling the suture through the muscle to tighten the suture line. In this large defect, carbon dioxide is released to reduce intra-abdominal pressure, allowing the fascial edges to be approximated. The decrease in space makes instrument manipulation challenging, so the surgeon alternates between placing sutures and adjusting insufflation pressure to maintain adequate working space.

The surgeon uses mobile needle holders to pass the needle between the right and left hands, facilitating suture placement. The suture line is tightened, pulling the muscle edges together, with external compression applied to the abdominal wall by the assistant to aid in approximation. The surgeon works from the inferior margin toward the initial suture line, running the defect closed from both ends to meet in the middle. Visual cues are used to monitor pressure on the suture and tissues due to the lack of tactile feedback in robotic surgery.

The surgeon has nearly completed the suture line, reaching the superior suture and crossing over the two suture lines. The V-lock suture visibly holds tension without a knot. The needle is removed and placed in the peritoneum to prevent misplacement, followed by tying a knot between the superior and inferior suture ends.

The surgeon places mesh over the primary repair for reinforcement. A purling suture is secured to the superior and inferior portions of the mesh. The bedside assistant uses a suture passer to bring the suture tails through the abdominal wall, where they will be tied in the subcutaneous space for trans-fascial fixation.

The surgeon holds the mesh against the underside of the fascia while the bedside assistant places absorbable tacks around its circumference to secure it. The surgeon points out the locations for tack placement, demonstrating the use of robotic suturing techniques as an alternative method for securing the mesh.
The surgeon completes the repair of the incisional hernia, ensuring a secure closure of the defect.
